[1] HIV Testing -- Diagnostic Tests -- Screening Procedure
Of note, 4th generation assays can produce a false positive Ag/Ab, which is known to be false by the lack of both HIV 1/2Ab and a confirmatory RNA test. If the first step, Ag/Ab is positive, the test must be completed in its entirety with an HIV 1/2Ab and RNA test that results in a positive or negative final result. In the scenario of only partial results from a 4th-generation test (due to an insufficient sample), the results are not valid. See Figure 1.[CDC Stacks. 2018 Quick reference guide: Recommended laboratory HIV testing algorithm for serum or plasma specimens ]. Point-of-care testing for HIV continues to evolve in clinical practice. Currently, there is one single-use, rapid point-of-care assay capable of detecting both HIV-1 p24 antigen and antibodies to HIV-1/2. This test, performed with a fingerstick specimen, is CLIA-waived and identifies HIV infection earlier than antibody-only rapid tests. However, its sensitivity during early infection remains lower than that of the recommended laboratory-based, fourth-generation serum HIV screen. While several rapid antibody-only point-of-care tests are available, they are not routinely recommended in clinical care. Nonetheless, these tests may be valuable in specific circumstances, such as occupational exposures or when women present in labor without documented prenatal HIV screening. [9]
